content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
428 J Street
Sacramento, CA 958 12-0807
(916) 322-5660
Fax (916) 322-0886
Mattie Taormina
Special Assistant to the State Librarian
Library-Courts Building
P.O. Box 942827
Sacramento, Ca 94237-000 1
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-220
Dear Ms. Taormina:
September 14, 1999
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the statement of economic
interests disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTION
Must members of the California Gold Discovery to Statehood Sesquicentennial
Commission (Sesquicentennial Commission) file leaving office statements of economic interests
as a result of the defunding of the Commission?
CONCLUSION
Unless they resign their positions on the Sesquicentennial Commission, they are not
required to file leaving office statements of economic interests at this time.
Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, 18109-18955, of the California Code of
Regulations.


Mattie Taormina
Our File No. A-99-220
Page 2
FACTS
You are writing on behalf of Dr. Kevin Starr, State Librarian of California, and the
Chairman ofthe California Gold Discovery to Statehood Sesquicentennial Commission.
The Sesquicentennial Commission adopted a conflict of interest code, effective as of
January 20, 1999, which designates its members as having an obligation to file statements of
economic interests.
The Sesquicentennial Commission was created by Executive Order W-74-94 on
January 24, 1994, and will cease to exist at the end of 2001.
However, the Sesquicentennial
Commission was defunded as of July 1, 1999.
The Sesquicentennial Commission was initially provided staff support by the Department
of Parks and Recreation. Later that support was provided by the California State Library and
then by the Office ofthe Secretary of State up to June 30, 1999. You have provided an extract
from the 1999-2000 State Budget showing that the Secretary of State will no longer provide
support services to the Sesquicentennial Commission. It is our understanding those services
henceforth will again be provided by the Department of Parks and Recreation.
You have also provided a copy of a letter sent to members of the Sesquicentennial
Commission from Dr. Starr, indicating that “recent action by the Joint Legislative Budget
Committee will almost certainly result in dissolution of the California Sesquicentennial
Commission.”
ANALYSIS
Regulation 18730(b)(5)(D) provides that all persons who leave designated positions shall
file a leaving office statement of economic interests within 30 days after leaving office.
The Executive Order which created the Sesquicentennial Commission indicates it will
cease to exist at the end of 2001. Therefore, notwithstanding its defunding, the Sesquicentennial
Commission will exist until the end of the year 2001, unless another Executive Order is issued
which terminates it at an earlier date. Though the Commission has no funding of its own, it does
have ongoing support services.
Unless members of the Sesquicentennial Commission resign their positions, they will not
leave office until the Sesquicentennial Commission sunsets. If they do not resign their positions,
they must continue to file annual statements every year and they would conclude their filing
obligations by filing a combined annual and leaving office statement by January 30, 2002.


Mattie Taormina
Our File No. A-99-220
Page 3
However, if members of the Commission submit resignations to the appointing power,
they will be obligated to file a leaving office statement within 30 days after submission oftheir
resignations.
5660.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
SGC:WPI:wi
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General
By:
Reform Consultant II
cc: Mr. Robert Jennings
